EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated thunderstorm which produced a severe wind gust moved across Custer county.

A very unstable atmosphere was in place across far Southeast Montana during the late evening hours of the 18th into the early morning hours of the 19th. CAPES were on the order of 2000-3000 J/KG along with a low-level jet around 45 kts. All the above, combined with a weak mid-level impulse, helped to generate severe thunderstorms across portions of South Central and Southeast Montana.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist easterly surface flow brought in fairly high dewpoints across the Billings Forecast Area. These dewpoints, combined with steep lapse rates, yielded an area of 1500-2500 J/KG of CAPE. In addition, a low-level jet worked its way into Southeast Montana. This all contributed to a few strong and severe thunderstorms across South Central and Southeast Montana.

A deep trough of low pressure over the West Coast caused strong and energetic southwest flow aloft to move over Montana. As a series of weather disturbances embedded within this trough pushed through, high winds developed initially to areas along the Rocky Mountain Front, then spread into the north-central plains and portions of southwest Montana. Light rain and snow also occurred over higher terrain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cut Bank ASOS measures 76 mph wind gust 3 miles SW Cut Bank.

The first major warm-up of the season earlier this month was enough to begin breaking up the ice and start the creeks running, which impacted a some of the smaller drainage basins across northeast Montana through mid-month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A warmer-than-normal start to the month of March allowed area creeks and drainages to freely run as the snowpack quickly melted.  As the Mid Muddy Creek flowed southward through Sheridan County, the rate of snowmelt became too much for the creek channel to handle.  It rose above the flood stage of 12 feet early in the evening of March 12th, crested at 14.09 feet on the morning of March 13th, as fell back down below flood stage later in the morning of March 12th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first major warm-up of the season earlier this month was enough to begin breaking up the ice and start the creeks running, which impacted a some of the smaller drainage basins across northeast Montana through mid-month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A warmer-than-normal start to the month of March allowed the ice on the Poplar River to break up.  The ice breaks jammed up and began to flood the Poplar River near the town of Poplar.  The river rose above the flood stage of 15.5 feet during the afternoon of March 13th, crested at 16.35 feet later in the early morning of March 14th, and fell back down below flood stage in the early morning of March 15th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic front moved into the Billings Forecast Area during the late evening hours on the 2nd. Precipitation initially fell as light rain then freezing rain as temperatures dropped during the late evening hours on the 2nd into the early morning hours of the 3rd. The rain then changed to snow and accumulated on already icy road surfaces. Icy road conditions continued for the next week to ten days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall around 5 inches was reported along with strong winds resulting in reduced visibilities of a quarter mile at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unusually warm temperatures for this time of year allowed stronger winds from above to mix down to the surface, enhancing the chinook wind effect from the west, especially near the lee side of the Little Rocky Mountains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A favorable weather pattern amid a ridge of high pressure allowed stronger winds from above to mix down to the surface, enhancing the chinook wind effect from the west, especially near the lee side of the Little Rocky Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Malta South US-191 DOT site recorded sustained winds of 40 mph or greater generally from 2:45 pm on the 8th to 1:10 am on the 9th.  The first verifying wind gust of 59 mph was measured at 3:30 pm on the 8th and the peak wind gust of 67 mph was measured from 6:43 to 6:45 pm, then again from 8:00 to 8:15 pm on the 8th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of above normal temperatures for this time of year allowed stronger winds from above to mix down to the surface, enhancing the chinook wind effect from the west, especially near the lee side of the Little Rocky Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Malta South US-191 DOT site measured sustained winds of 40 mph or greater from 1:34 to 6:30 am on the 8th, with a first verifying gust of 58 mph at 2:24 am and a peak wind gust of 61 mph at 5:45 am.

Heavy rain and small hail producing thunderstorms developed the afternoon of May 30th, 2015. The strongest cell was a supercell in Western Montana which held together for over 2 hours and produced nickel-sized hail and torrential downpours over I-90 west of Superior, MT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS Employee experienced torrential rainfall and 0.50 inch hail while driving on I-90. The hail lasted 5 min, and rain rates were so high cars on the 75 mph interstate slowed to around 25 mph for a few minutes due to reduced visibility. A nearby RAWS weather station reported 0.55 inches of rain from the storm shortly before it crossed I-90.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low freezing levels combined with a decent updraft resulted in a thunderstorm that produced hail just over an inch in diameter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front brought hot, dry and breezy conditions to the fire on August 28th and 29th. The cold front eventually moved through during the afternoon of August 29th and brought gusty winds to the wildfire. The fire grew from 2,922 acres to just under 12,000 acres by Saturday afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Forest service employee reported that 2 forest service structures were burned from the fire.  Total cost of the fire is estimated at 5 million dollars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level disturbance and associated cold front moved rapidly across the Northern Rockies on Saturday March 28th. The system produced widespread severe wind, with peak wind gusts ranging from 60 to 70 mph, along with critical fire weather conditions. The severe winds hit at the prime time of the day when temperatures were at record levels and humidities were in the teens. As a result, several wildfires occurred during the afternoon and early evening. The event was preceded by a late winter and early spring of below normal precipitation and above normal temperatures which set up unseasonably dry and dead fuel conditions. The high winds resulted in numerous power outages along with minor home/roof and tree damage.||Two wildfires that resulted in the greatest impacts were located near Red Lodge Mountain, as well as the Pinecrest Subdivision located between Park City and Columbus, Montana. The Pinecrest Wildfire (near Park City, Montana) burned 3000 acres. Two homes were lost and one firefighter was injured (minor).  In addition, Interstate 90 between Park City, MT and Columbus MT (20 miles) was closed for several hours due to low visibilities due to smoke. However, before the interstate closed, a multi-vehicle accident occurred, resulting in 1 fatality and several other injuries.||The Red Lodge Wildfire, which started shortly after 12 pm,  charred about 600 acres and threatened homes near Red Lodge as skiers were escorted off the mountain. The fire started when high winds fanned a hot spot from a controlled burn in the Land Estates Subdivision from the previous Wednesday. Fire officials closed Ski Run Road while they battled the blaze, which jumped West Fork Road. No one was allowed to enter or leave Red Lodge Mountain. Around 3 pm, skiers in their vehicles were escorted off the mountain by Carbon County officials.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Pinecrest Wildfire, 4 miles northeast of Columbus, Montana burned 3000 acres. Two homes were lost and one firefighter was injured (minor). In addition, Interstate 90 between Park City, MT and Columbus MT (20 miles) was closed for several hours due to low visibilities due to smoke from the wildfire. However, before the interstate closed, a multi-vehicle accident occurred, resulting in 1 fatality and several other injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving thunderstorm which produced very heavy rain along with significant amounts of small hail resulted in flash flooding across the Shepherd area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A very heavy and slow moving thunderstorm resulted in flash flooding just north of Shepherd. Radar estimated 3 to 5 inches of rain fell in about an hours time. Significant amounts of fast moving water flowed across portions of Pleasant Hollow Trail which resulted in some of the road being eroded. In addition, significant amounts of small hail, resembling large snow drifts, piled up in several places.

This all resulted in a few isolated, but very severe, thunderstorms across the central and southeast portions of the Billings Forecast Area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system developed over the Great Basin and tracked northeastward into central and eastern Montana the first week of November. Heaviest snow amounts were in the mountains but the storm's impacts at lower elevations included power outages in Bozeman and numerous vehicle slide offs on area roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nine inches of snow fell at the Boulder Mountain SNOTEL site (elevation 7950 feet).

A supercell thunderstorm caused significant hail damage in the Sidney, MT area.  As the storm moved southeast, a tornado formed in a rural area and traveled into North Dakota.  The tornado injured a 79 year old man.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist and unstable atmosphere combined with an upper-level disturbance to allow a few strong to severe thunderstorms to develop over McCone and Garfield counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A metal pole barn with wooden frame sustained damage from thunderstorm outflow winds, including the roof being blown off, and the north and south walls collapsing.  Owner was not present to witness the damage.  Event time is estimated from radar imagery. Through discussion and photo analysis of the damage, the peak wind speed is estimated at 95 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter measured 83 mph winds. A power pole and a 7 foot high cattle implement were blown over.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front and a moist and unstable atmosphere provided the focus for strong thunderstorms to develop and move through far eastern Montana. A supercell thunderstorm caused significant hail damage in the Sidney, MT area.  As the storm moved southeast, a tornado formed in a rural area and traveled into North Dakota.  The tornado injured a 79 year old man.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The National Weather Service in Glasgow, along with Richland County Disaster and Emergency Services conducted a damage survey of a property.  The mobile home and its associated storage building (also a mobile home) were thrown from its foundation. The primary home lost its metal frame and was thrown onto a storage shed to the southwest, about 60 feet to the southwest. The two mobile homes, which did not appear to have been strapped down, were overturned and one had the entire frame bent and twisted. While there were tree branches broken in the region, the most significant damage was on this property site, due to the rural nature of the area.  Power poles on the property were not damaged, although the lines were snapped.  ||One 79 year old gentleman was in the home with his dog also on the property.  The gentleman was in the mobile home and suffered injuries requiring transport to a local and then regional hospital.   The dog was found trapped under rubble but was ok. ||Based off of the damage that was observed, the winds were estimated at 100 mph.     The tornado continued through rural area to the south-southeast where it went into North Dakota about 5 miles south-southeast of the property site with the damage.  No reported damage has been noted in North Dakota and the tornado dissipated south-southwest of Trotters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Reynolds Creek Fire began around 3:45 pm on Tuesday, July 21. It was in an area approximately six miles east of Logan Pass and four miles west of the Saint Mary Visitor Center. The fire burned thousands of trees along Going-to-the-Sun Road, two structures were lost, including the historic Baring Creek Cabin and an old homestead along Saint Mary Lake that was constructed prior to the creation of the park. A vehicle was also destroyed in the fire. The cost of the fire was approximately 10.1 million as of August 7, 2015 and the cause of the fire is under investigation. Resources from Flathead National Forest, Glacier County, East Glacier, Babb, St. Mary, Cutbank, Evergreen, and West Valley Fire Departments, Blackfeet Fire Management, Montana Department of Natural Resources, Flathead County, as well as Glacier County and Montana Disaster & Emergency Services assisted Glacier National Park in the fire incident. The final update on the Reynolds Creek Fire was issued on August 7, 2015.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Reynolds Creek Fire began around 3:45 pm on Tuesday, July 21. It was in an area approximately six miles east of Logan Pass and four miles west of the Saint Mary Visitor Center. The fire burned thousands of trees along Going-to-the-Sun Road, two structures were lost, including the historic Baring Creek Cabin and an old homestead along Saint Mary Lake that was constructed prior to the creation of the park. A vehicle was also destroyed in the fire. The cost of the fire was approximately 10.1 million as of August 7, 2015 and the cause of the fire is under investigation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary along with moderate to strong buoyancy was situated over the northern high plains. This boundary was associated with a strong pressure rise-fall couplet from west to east across portions of southern Montana and northern Wyoming. This resulted in strong forcing and ascent across the area. This, combined with 30-45 kts of deep shear, resulted in organized/sustained severe thunderstorms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very active and potent mid-level trough, sweeping through the northern Rockies, met atmospheric conditions over northeast Montana which allowed the stronger winds from above to mix down to the surface.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds of at least 40 mph were common during the early to mid-afternoon hours.  The peak wind gusts of 60 mph occurred at 15:08 MST at the Beaver Hill I-94 DOT site and from 13:15 to 13:55 MST at the Beach, ND ASOS site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very active and potent mid-level trough, sweeping through the northern Rockies, met atmospheric conditions over northeast Montana which allowed the stronger winds from above to mix down to the surface.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple locations measured sustained and gusting winds near and above 40 mph through most of the day.  The peak wind gust of 60 mph was recorded by the Glendive AWOS at 13:35 MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary along with moderate to strong buoyancy was situated over the northern high plains. This boundary was associated with a strong pressure rise-fall couplet from west to east across portions of southern Montana and northern Wyoming. This resulted in strong forcing and ascent across the area. This, combined with 30-45 kts of deep shear, resulted in organized/sustained severe thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter estimated a 60 mph wind gust.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported a tree down due to the strong thunderstorm winds.

A cold front moved south over the hi-line late in the day July 4th.  This caused enhancement of thunderstorms in a warm and moist airmass after peak heating. These elevated storms produced damaging winds and large hail for a large swath land near the Canadian Border. Damage from the event was estimated at $7.5 million.  An article covering the cost of the storm from local media can be seen at http://www.ktvh.com/home/headlines/Hailstorms-Cause-Millions-in-Damage-to-Wheat-Grain-Crops--318469291.html .
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Department of Transportation roadside station recorded a 50 knot wind gust.

A storm system developed over the Great Basin and tracked northeastward into central and eastern Montana the first week of November. Heaviest snow amounts were in the mountains but the storm's impacts at lower elevations included power outages in Bozeman and numerous vehicle slide offs on area roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong westerly flow aloft developed along the U.S. - Canadian border and was able to mix down to the surface. The resulting high winds overturned a semi tractor-trailer on U.S. Highway 2 near Browning, snapped one inch tree limbs, tore shingles off of homes and outbuildings, and caused power outages. Wind gusts in excess of 80 mph were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust to 62 mph was reported at Cut Bank 3SW (the Cut Bank ASOS site).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front and a weak to moderately-unstable atmosphere combined to form strong to severe thunderstorms across portions of northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Glasgow Airport ASOS site measured a 58 mph wind gust.  No impacts were reported from this wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moist surface conditions, an unstable atmosphere, and a low pressure trough combined to form and sustain multiple organized severe thunderstorms across portions of northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large, four-year old roping barn was destroyed by a microburst. The winds peeled the metal roof back, the tresses collapsed, and the east wall was blown eastward.  Based on photographs from the Emergency Manager, and archived radar data, the maximum wind gusts from the microburst were estimated at 95 mph.

The hardest hit area was Powder River County where eleven power poles were knocked down between Ashland and Broadus resulting in power outages for several days.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported dime-sized hail, 30-40 mph winds, brief wind gusts of 50 mph along with heavy rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front and a weak to moderately-unstable atmosphere combined to form strong to severe thunderstorms across portions of northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported 5 power poles snapped by thunderstorm winds.  Time based on radar velocity data at this location.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line moved across portions of Central and South Central Montana during the late afternoon and early evening hours of the 1st. This squall line produced some large hail but mainly damaging winds. The hardest hit area was Powder River County where eleven power poles were knocked down between Ashland and Broadus resulting in power outages for several days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eleven power poles were snapped along Highway 212 between Ashland and Broadus. This resulted in power outages that lasted two days.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An exceptionally strong atmospheric river brought up to 3 inches of precipitation and well-above normal temperatures to the region. Record-breaking temperatures into the 50s occurred in many valley locations. An unusually high number of roadway rockslides was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused a rockslide on February 7th that closed the westbound lanes of I-90 west of St Regis, MT. Several hundred small rocks and boulders had to be cleared from the road surface. No accidents were reported. Westbound lanes were closed for 2 days as traffic was forced to divert to the north via MT Hwy 135 and MT Hwy 200, thus increasing travel distance. By the afternoon of Feb 9th, one lane, two-way traffic had been set-up, ending the interstate re-route.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another upper level disturbance moved across the area with low level moisture allowing for enhanced storm development and decent rainfall rates.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail size reported from observer after storm had passed.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall amount of 1.30 inches in less than one hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong jet stream moved across Southwest and Central Montana during the afternoon of the 6th into the early morning hours of the 7th. Conditions became favorable for the very strong winds within the jet stream to mix down not only to mountain top level but down the eastern foothills and adjacent plains of the Beartooth/Absaroka Mountains. ||Sustained winds of 40 to 50 mph with gusts of 60 to 75 mph were common across the the foothills and areas adjacent for an extended period of time. The Stillwater Mine  estimated winds at 90 mph. Numerous power outages and downed trees were reported from Red Lodge to Big Timber. A downed power pole about 5 miles southwest of Fishtail started a grass fire on Nye Road. The fire burned 60 to 70 acres. In addition, some car windows in the Stillwater Mine parking lot were damaged, as well as window damage to a home on Benbow. ||Another downed power line sparked a fire north of the town of Big Timber just across the Yellowstone River. The fire occurred about three miles west of Featherbed Road in Sweet Grass County. The fire started about 820 pm and burned about 10 acres, mostly cottonwood trees and some river bottom foliage. However, one primary structure and two outbuildings burned. No one occupied the buildings but the structures did contain farm equipment.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 70 mph was recorded in Roscoe with a 60 mph gust at Tipi Village in Red Lodge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long duration snow event began Saturday evening Jan. 3 while snow intensities increased on Jan. 4 to produce widespread high impacts across western Montana and central Idaho. An Arctic front pushed west of the Continental Divide stalling from just south of Troy extending across Mineral County and the Missoula Valley and then southeast to Deer Lodge on Jan. 5. Areas along and north of this boundary helped to focus the moderate to heavy snowfall on Sunday. The plume of moisture with origins from the tropics, ran over the arctic air in place to cause very heavy amounts of snowfall across northwest Montana. As the front retreated northward on Jan. 5 locations across west-central Montana saw freezing rain that caused untreated surfaces to become slick.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After 6 inches of snow fell in the Missoula area, freezing rain developed and caused up to 0.25 of glaze to form on untreated surfaces. Numerous school delays or cancellations were made for the Missoula and Bitterroot Valley school systems. Roads were especially bad due to the fact that previously plowed streets became very slick with the glaze of ice on them. Buses were not able to drive off the main roads in Florence and Victor due to the icy roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong westerly flow aloft developed along the U.S. - Canadian border and was able to mix down to the surface. The resulting high winds overturned a semi tractor-trailer on U.S. Highway 2 near Browning, snapped one inch tree limbs, tore shingles off of homes and outbuildings, and caused power outages. Wind gusts in excess of 80 mph were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust to 82 mph was reported at the Heart Butte RAWS site. Additionally, a wind gust to 91 mph was reported at Babb.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long duration snow event began Saturday evening Jan. 3 while snow intensities increased on Jan. 4 to produce widespread high impacts across western Montana and central Idaho. An Arctic front pushed west of the Continental Divide stalling from just south of Troy extending across Mineral County and the Missoula Valley and then southeast to Deer Lodge on Jan. 5. Areas along and north of this boundary helped to focus the moderate to heavy snowfall on Sunday. The plume of moisture with origins from the tropics, ran over the arctic air in place to cause very heavy amounts of snowfall across northwest Montana. As the front retreated northward on Jan. 5 locations across west-central Montana saw freezing rain that caused untreated surfaces to become slick.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Glacier Park International Airport reported 19.2 inches of storm total snowfall which became the 6th highest 2-day snowfall on record. Several families were stranded at their homes until they were plowed out because of the heavy snow. Most schools closed on Jan. 5 and also on Jan. 6 except for portions of the Mission Valley where 2-hour delays were common. The Kalispell City Manager reported that there were some roads that were impassable without 4WD vehicles. Weather spotters reported trees down in Bigfork and also over Highways 35 and 83. Other notable storm total snowfall totals include: 26.5 3 miles north of Bigfork, 23 Woods Bay, 21.5 Bigfork, and 8.5 4.1 miles northeast of Ronan.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: MDT reported severe driving conditions from Superior to St. Regis and Lookout Pass during the late evening on Jan. 4. They reported that it was so heavy at times that the snow plow drivers couldn't keep up. Chains were required over Evaro Hill for towing vehicles from roughly 5 am to 9 am MST on Jan. 5 which is a rare occurrence. Storm total snowfall at Lookout Pass was 21 inches while Heron saw 17 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An exceptionally strong atmospheric river brought up to 3 inches of precipitation and well-above normal temperatures to the region. Record-breaking temperatures into the 50s occurred in many valley locations. An unusually high number of roadway rockslides was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains led to a large boulder falling and blocking the west-bound lane of US-12 during the early morning hours of February 10th, 2015. The slide happened 14 miles west of Lolo, MT. The mountain pass road was closed to any through-traffic for over a day and a half as officials hired a crew to inspect, blast, and remove large rocks. Truckers reported losing thousands of dollars per day as they could not use the route. Road crews noted that this is one of the largest slides they could recall. Full traffic flow resumed 2 days later.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the Martin Luther King Jr. holiday, an upper level trough initiated several bands of rain and snow showers across the Northern Rockies. A long and transient snow band produced snowfall over Interstate 90 between Drummond and Deer Lodge which melted and froze after sundown. This created a sheet of ice that caused at least seven accidents with no injuries and shut down the highway for three hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Montana Highway Patrol reported road conditions were so slick that tires on some vehicles could not get traction and they became stranded. Traffic backed up from approximately 6:54 pm through 10 pm MST because of the accidents. A Greyhound bus and a number of semi-trucks jack-knifed across the highway while several vehicles ended up in the ditch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long duration snow event began Saturday evening Jan. 3 while snow intensities increased on Jan. 4 to produce widespread high impacts across western Montana and central Idaho. An Arctic front pushed west of the Continental Divide stalling from just south of Troy extending across Mineral County and the Missoula Valley and then southeast to Deer Lodge on Jan. 5. Areas along and north of this boundary helped to focus the moderate to heavy snowfall on Sunday. The plume of moisture with origins from the tropics, ran over the arctic air in place to cause very heavy amounts of snowfall across northwest Montana. As the front retreated northward on Jan. 5 locations across west-central Montana saw freezing rain that caused untreated surfaces to become slick.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The MDT estimated 18 inches of snow fell at Lolo Pass through the morning of Jan. 5. Snow changed to rain by the afternoon and by nightfall road surfaces became slick again. The MDT foreman in charge the Lolo Pass stretch mentioned that a small car would have had a tough time due to the very heavy snowfall rates. A 43-year-old man was killed on Highway 12 west of Lolo when his vehicle hit ice on a curve. Other factors involved included alcohol and not wearing his seat-belt.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tightened pressure gradient along the Rocky Mountain Front caused strong winds to develop and become gusty. The strongest winds occurred along the Northern Rocky Mountain Front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High wind gusts, measured at 60KT (69MPH), were reported 1 mi E of East Glacier Park, MT at the Two Medicine Bridge DOT sensor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tightened pressure gradient along the Rocky Mountain Front caused strong winds to develop and become gusty. The strongest winds occurred along the Northern Rocky Mountain Front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High wind gusts, measured at 62KT (71MPH), were reported 13 mi W of Bynum, MT at the Dellwo sensor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple skier triggered avalanches were reported in South Central Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two avalanches were reported:|1/13/2015	Northern Bridgers - Frazier - skier triggered, not caught.||1/14/2015	Northern Gallatin Range - Elephant - skier triggered, not caught.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple skier triggered avalanches were reported in Southwest Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One avalanche was reported:||1/19/2015	S. Gallatin	1 skier triggered, remote, not caught.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unsettled northwesterly flow brought off and on snow to Central Montana. Multiple shortwaves passing through the region caused string winds and widespread accumulating snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow, measuring 10.0 inches in 24 hours, was reported 10 mi SSE of Neihart, MT at the Deadman Creek SNOTEL; elevation 6450ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level disturbance and associated cold front moved rapidly across the Northern Rockies on Saturday March 28th. The system produced widespread severe wind, with peak wind gusts ranging from 60 to 70 mph, along with critical fire weather conditions. The severe winds hit at the prime time of the day when temperatures were at record levels and humidities were in the teens. As a result, several wildfires occurred during the afternoon and early evening. The event was preceded by a late winter and early spring of below normal precipitation and above normal temperatures which set up unseasonably dry and dead fuel conditions. The high winds resulted in numerous power outages along with minor home/roof and tree damage.||Two wildfires that resulted in the greatest impacts were located near Red Lodge Mountain, as well as the Pinecrest Subdivision located between Park City and Columbus, Montana. The Pinecrest Wildfire (near Park City, Montana) burned 3000 acres. Two homes were lost and one firefighter was injured (minor).  In addition, Interstate 90 between Park City, MT and Columbus MT (20 miles) was closed for several hours due to low visibilities due to smoke. However, before the interstate closed, a multi-vehicle accident occurred, resulting in 1 fatality and several other injuries.||The Red Lodge Wildfire, which started shortly after 12 pm,  charred about 600 acres and threatened homes near Red Lodge as skiers were escorted off the mountain. The fire started when high winds fanned a hot spot from a controlled burn in the Land Estates Subdivision from the previous Wednesday. Fire officials closed Ski Run Road while they battled the blaze, which jumped West Fork Road. No one was allowed to enter or leave Red Lodge Mountain. Around 3 pm, skiers in their vehicles were escorted off the mountain by Carbon County officials.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A gust of 59 mph was recorded at the Baker airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level disturbance and associated cold front moved rapidly across the Northern Rockies on Saturday March 28th. The system produced widespread severe wind, with peak wind gusts ranging from 60 to 70 mph, along with critical fire weather conditions. The severe winds hit at the prime time of the day when temperatures were at record levels and humidities were in the teens. As a result, several wildfires occurred during the afternoon and early evening. The event was preceded by a late winter and early spring of below normal precipitation and above normal temperatures which set up unseasonably dry and dead fuel conditions. The high winds resulted in numerous power outages along with minor home/roof and tree damage.||Two wildfires that resulted in the greatest impacts were located near Red Lodge Mountain, as well as the Pinecrest Subdivision located between Park City and Columbus, Montana. The Pinecrest Wildfire (near Park City, Montana) burned 3000 acres. Two homes were lost and one firefighter was injured (minor).  In addition, Interstate 90 between Park City, MT and Columbus MT (20 miles) was closed for several hours due to low visibilities due to smoke. However, before the interstate closed, a multi-vehicle accident occurred, resulting in 1 fatality and several other injuries.||The Red Lodge Wildfire, which started shortly after 12 pm,  charred about 600 acres and threatened homes near Red Lodge as skiers were escorted off the mountain. The fire started when high winds fanned a hot spot from a controlled burn in the Land Estates Subdivision from the previous Wednesday. Fire officials closed Ski Run Road while they battled the blaze, which jumped West Fork Road. No one was allowed to enter or leave Red Lodge Mountain. Around 3 pm, skiers in their vehicles were escorted off the mountain by Carbon County officials.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts of 66 to 73 mph were recorded at the Billings Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep trough of low pressure over the West Coast caused strong and energetic southwest flow aloft to move over Montana. As a series of weather disturbances embedded within this trough pushed through, high winds developed initially to areas along the Rocky Mountain Front, then spread into the north-central plains and portions of southwest Montana. Light rain and snow also occurred over higher terrain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High wind gust of 68 mph measured at 4 miles W Valier.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A small but active upper-level disturbance from the northern Rockies and record high temperatures ahead of a strong cold front created an environment which allowed damaging winds from above to reach the surface, creating a squall line and a few thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple public reports, along highway 16 near the town of Savage, of less than 1/4 mile visibility in blowing dirt, likely due to recently plowed fields and strong frontal winds. Power pole was snapped along highway 16 in Sidney. A 66 mph wind gust was recorded at the Sioux Pass MT-16 DOT site at 4:01 pm local time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A small but active upper-level disturbance from the northern Rockies and record high temperatures ahead of a strong cold front created an environment which allowed damaging winds from above to reach the surface, creating a squall line and a few thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: First verifying wind gust of 59 mph was recorded at the Cow Creek MT-13 DOT site at 2:54 pm local time.  Maximum wind gust of 65 mph was recorded at the Fort Peck Dam handar mesonet site at 3:15 pm local time.  A separate public report was received of a stack of tin sheets scattered and blown a few hundred yards away. Winds diminished back below criteria levels by 3:30 pm local time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long duration snow event began Saturday evening Jan. 3 while snow intensities increased on Jan. 4 to produce widespread high impacts across western Montana and central Idaho. An Arctic front pushed west of the Continental Divide stalling from just south of Troy extending across Mineral County and the Missoula Valley and then southeast to Deer Lodge on Jan. 5. Areas along and north of this boundary helped to focus the moderate to heavy snowfall on Sunday. The plume of moisture with origins from the tropics, ran over the arctic air in place to cause very heavy amounts of snowfall across northwest Montana. As the front retreated northward on Jan. 5 locations across west-central Montana saw freezing rain that caused untreated surfaces to become slick.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The MDT reported that a snow slide affected Highway 83, two miles north of Swan Lake. Except for traffic being diverted to one lane, no further information was provided on the size of the avalanche.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long duration snow event began Saturday evening Jan. 3 while snow intensities increased on Jan. 4 to produce widespread high impacts across western Montana and central Idaho. An Arctic front pushed west of the Continental Divide stalling from just south of Troy extending across Mineral County and the Missoula Valley and then southeast to Deer Lodge on Jan. 5. Areas along and north of this boundary helped to focus the moderate to heavy snowfall on Sunday. The plume of moisture with origins from the tropics, ran over the arctic air in place to cause very heavy amounts of snowfall across northwest Montana. As the front retreated northward on Jan. 5 locations across west-central Montana saw freezing rain that caused untreated surfaces to become slick.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The MDT reported that between 11 to 16 inches of snow fell between Marion and Happy's Inn, up to 20 inches between Libby and Happy's Inn on US-2. They reported that the snow changed to freezing rain at times later on Jan. 5 through the early morning hours of Jan. 6. The Lincoln County Emergency Manager reported a few downed power lines and brief power outages. A weather spotter just southwest of Libby measured 18 inches of snow while Troy receive 11 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long duration snow event began Saturday evening Jan. 3 while snow intensities increased on Jan. 4 to produce widespread high impacts across western Montana and central Idaho. An Arctic front pushed west of the Continental Divide stalling from just south of Troy extending across Mineral County and the Missoula Valley and then southeast to Deer Lodge on Jan. 5. Areas along and north of this boundary helped to focus the moderate to heavy snowfall on Sunday. The plume of moisture with origins from the tropics, ran over the arctic air in place to cause very heavy amounts of snowfall across northwest Montana. As the front retreated northward on Jan. 5 locations across west-central Montana saw freezing rain that caused untreated surfaces to become slick.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The BNSF avalanche forecaster reported a storm total snowfall in Essex of 20.4 inches with 1.40 inches of snow water equivalent. They reported that the snow changed to freezing rain on Jan. 6 and then the second push of arctic air froze everything up solid. A large avalanche covered US-2 10 miles north of Essex while a second avalanche occurred around 920 am on Jan 5, two miles north of Swan Lake. The West Glacier and West Valley school systems were closed on Jan. 6.  Blowing snow was reported at Bad Rock Canyon and at Marias Pass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep plume of Pacific moisture overran a cold airmass east of the Continental Divide resulting in areas of heavy snow just before the Thanksgiving holiday. Snow amounts in excess of 6 inches were common. Gusty winds created areas of blowing and drifting snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roads were snow-covered and icy causing hazardous travel conditions during a high-impact period of the Thanksgiving holiday travel period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long duration snow event began Saturday evening Jan. 3 while snow intensities increased on Jan. 4 to produce widespread high impacts across western Montana and central Idaho. An Arctic front pushed west of the Continental Divide stalling from just south of Troy extending across Mineral County and the Missoula Valley and then southeast to Deer Lodge on Jan. 5. Areas along and north of this boundary helped to focus the moderate to heavy snowfall on Sunday. The plume of moisture with origins from the tropics, ran over the arctic air in place to cause very heavy amounts of snowfall across northwest Montana. As the front retreated northward on Jan. 5 locations across west-central Montana saw freezing rain that caused untreated surfaces to become slick.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The MDT reported an avalanche that blocked both lanes of US-2 located 10 miles west of Essex near the Blue Rock Cut or at milepost 168.6. It was approximately 30 to 40 yards long, 3 feet deep on down hill side, and 5 feet deep on the uphill side. One lane was opened up after 1.5 hours while the other lane took an additional hour to clean up.

They also reported that the power went out starting at 6 pm MST on Jan. 4. At one point Highway 200 east bound lanes east of Bonner were closed for several hours due to a snow slide. MDT also reported severe driving conditions along Highway 200 due to icy conditions and freezing rain. The Seeley-Swan school system was closed on Jan. 6 because of the freezing rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level disturbance and associated cold front moved rapidly across the Northern Rockies on Saturday March 28th. The system produced widespread severe wind, with peak wind gusts ranging from 60 to 70 mph, along with critical fire weather conditions. The severe winds hit at the prime time of the day when temperatures were at record levels and humidities were in the teens. As a result, several wildfires occurred during the afternoon and early evening. The event was preceded by a late winter and early spring of below normal precipitation and above normal temperatures which set up unseasonably dry and dead fuel conditions. The high winds resulted in numerous power outages along with minor home/roof and tree damage.||Two wildfires that resulted in the greatest impacts were located near Red Lodge Mountain, as well as the Pinecrest Subdivision located between Park City and Columbus, Montana. Fire officials closed Ski Run Road while they battled the blaze, which jumped West Fork Road. No one was allowed to enter or leave Red Lodge Mountain. Around 3 pm, skiers in their vehicles were escorted off the mountain by Carbon County officials.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A multi-vehicle crash occurred on Interstate 90. A woman from Park City, Montana was killed after blowing smoke mixed with dirt/dust created zero visibility. The crash involved five vehicles and injured several people around 3 pm. According to the Montana Highway Patrol , speed and low visibility played a significant role in the crash. The crash occurred in the westbound lane near mile marker 432. A 67-year-old woman who died was a passenger in a Ford Edge pickup. Billings dispatch said the woman was dead on arrival.

A slow-moving and very cold Pacific trough, originating from eastern Siberia, moved into central Idaho and western Montana bringing with it snow and wind. Because the upper level jet orientation was southerly, many places saw an extended period of snow behind the cold front passage. Central Idaho saw the effects of this system first in the morning as moderate to heavy snow fell caused slushy roads. Then as the cold front marched eastward, conditions deteriorated across southwest Montana. This was a big change from the day before in which temperatures were in the 60s and low 70s.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Due to the nature of this system, it was a major change to road conditions and most likely caught some travelers off-guard. Three main areas saw the highest impacts with the storm: The first area was around Georgetown Lake. Snowplow crews barely could keep up with the heavy snowfall even with extra equipment they had to bring in to mitigate. The second area included the Racetrack Interchange to Warm Springs along Interstate 90. Heavy wet snow caused deep slush on the road which contributed to a three vehicle car accident in that stretch. The third area that saw impacts covered a stretch of I-90 east of Butte to Homestake Pass. Blizzard-like conditions were reported by the MDT and the sharp temperature drop from the 40s to the 20s caused road temperatures cold enough for snow to stick. Weather sensors reported wind gusts to 38 mph while snow amounts up to 6 inches were estimated in the higher terrain including Homestake Pass. Two jack-knifed vehicles caused Homestake Pass to be closed for a time around 415 pm MDT.

The one to two foot drifts formed continually throughout the evening especially between 830 and 1030 pm MST on highway 28 between the 10 to 19 mile-markers. Another impact was snow that melted then froze to an ice layer under the snow causing treacherous conditions. They received anywhere from 1 to 5 inches. The manager had to call in extra help from another area to mitigate the conditions.

The snow melted and then turned to ice as colder air filtered south causing road conditions to become very slick. The MDT and a NWS employee reported very icy roads during the morning of the 3rd. Alberton received 4.5 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong arctic cold front caused heavy snow, blowing snow and icy road surfaces due to cold temperatures across portions of western Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Even though a few places saw a melt and re-freeze scenario with slick roads, the majority of the Missoula and Bitterroot Valleys saw low impact. Around a half-inch of snow fell in Frenchtown which froze after sunset causing roads to slicken up. A 57-year-old man was injured in an accident in which he slid and rolled off I-90 near Frenchtown. The MDT reported periods of moderate snow with gusty winds and drifting snow in the Hamilton area.

The snow began around 5:20 pm on the 2nd at Lost Trail Pass and caused road conditions to become slick throughout the evening. Webcams revealed some blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Atmospheric conditions set up for favorable downslope nocturnal winds on the lee side of the Little Rocky Mountains, resulting in stronger than expected wind speeds until the early morning hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the Malta South US-191 DOT site, sustained winds of at least 40 mph began at 8:08 PM on Apr. 14th and continued until ending at 5:50 AM on Apr. 15th.  High winds verified at 10:08 PM on the 14th with at least 2 consecutive hours of 40 mph sustained winds.  Strongest wind gusts of 61 mph were recorded between 1:10 AM and 1:30 AM on the 15th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms developed at 3 pm MDT over Lemhi county and was associated with very frequent lightning strikes. This cluster of thunderstorms developed into a line and brought strong damaging winds to the Bitterroot and Missoula valleys. Numerous power lines were knocked down as a line of 60 mph winds push through the Bitterroot valley. As the thunderstorms approached Missoula, a macroburst developed and brought 74 mph wind gusts to the city of Missoula. Many power lines and trees were brought down and caused power outages for days in some areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines and trees were downed by the wind. One of the trees that was knocked over was 18 inches in diameter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low across northeast Wyoming and associated cold front/pool across southeast Montana was the focus mechanism for the severe thunderstorms. Dewpoints in the 50s to lower 60s along with daytime heating and steep lapse rates all contributed to destabilize the atmosphere. This all resulted in a few isolated, but very severe, thunderstorms across the central and southeast portions of the Billings Forecast Area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm producing hail and very heavy rain impacted the city of Billings. The 6th Street underpass closed due to high water. In addition, street flooding was reported at the intersection of 3rd and Grand Avenue, Grand Avenue  between 20th and 22nd street, as well as several other locations throughout the city.

As the thunderstorms approached Missoula, a macroburst developed and brought 74 mph wind gusts to the city of Missoula. Many power lines and trees were brought down and caused power outages for days in some areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A macroburst brought damaging winds to the city of Missoula. A 74 mph wind gust was measured at the Missoula airport, but a majority of the damage occurred towards the |South Hills area. Major power lines and large trees were knocked down, and some residents were without power for four days. A local power company reported that several power poles, rated at 80 mph, were snapped in half. Over a million dollars in damages is estimated for this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Southwest flow aloft combined with a strong upper level disturbance that moved over the Billings Forecast Area resulted in severe thunderstorms that produced large hail and very strong wind gusts.
A shortwave trough moved across the Billings Forecast Area. This, combined with some weak instability, resulted in a few severe thunderstorms.
A few strong thunderstorms moved across portions of South Central Montana. These thunderstorms only produced sub-severe hail.
A shortwave trough moved across the Billings Forecast Area. This, combined with some weak instability, resulted in a few severe thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail accumulated to around one inch on the ground. Some property damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist atmosphere combined with 30-40 Kts of deep layer shear allowed for moderate instability across Southeast Montana. This combined with a disturbance aloft resulted in severe thunderstorms across this area.

A deep plume of Pacific moisture overran a cold airmass east of the Continental Divide resulting in areas of heavy snow just before the Thanksgiving holiday. Snow amounts in excess of 6 inches were common. Gusty winds created areas of blowing and drifting snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous vehicle accidents due to icy and snow-covered roads between Wolf Creek and Helena as well as low visibility in heavy snow. KHLN reported less than 0.50 mile visibility between 1100 and 1600 LST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist easterly surface flow brought in fairly high dewpoints across the Billings Forecast Area. These dewpoints, combined with steep lapse rates, yielded an area of 1500-2500 J/KG of CAPE. In addition, a low-level jet worked its way into Southeast Montana. This all contributed to a few strong and severe thunderstorms across South Central and Southeast Montana.

A strong cold front, producing very gusty winds, swept across the entire Billings Forecast area on Sunday, October 11th. Some of the strongest wind gusts were around the Billings area where multiple reports of wind gusts around 70 mph were reported. The high winds wreaked havoc in Billings knocking out power to hundreds of homes and downing trees across town. The 70 mph gust measured at Billings Logan International Airport was the strongest recorded in central and eastern Montana. ||The strong winds caused at least one fire in Billings when a tree fell onto power lines near the intersection of Poly Drive and Hoover Avenue. The top of the downed tree burned briefly but the flames did not spread. The incident contributed to power outages for 800 to 1000 electric customers, mostly across the northwestern part of the city. Multiple power outages were reported in the Billings and Laurel areas throughout the day. The high winds also caused Zoo Montana to close after multiple trees fell on the property. In addition, minor house damage was also reported across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 60 mph was reported just southeast of Baker.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Across most of western Roosevelt County, wind speeds were not quite at warning criteria, but a peak wind gust of 63 mph was measured at the McDonalds MT-13 DOT site at 6:53 pm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A compact upper-level trough and strong cold front from the northern Rockies pushed dangerously gusty winds through most of northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The stronger sustained winds occurred generally during the afternoon and evening hours.  A peak wind gust of 71 mph was measured multiple times at the Comertown Turn-Off MT-5 DOT site from 4:39 pm to 7:03 pm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front, producing very gusty winds, swept across the entire Billings Forecast area on Sunday, October 11th. Some of the strongest wind gusts were around the Billings area where multiple reports of wind gusts around 70 mph were reported. The high winds wreaked havoc in Billings knocking out power to hundreds of homes and downing trees across town. The 70 mph gust measured at Billings Logan International Airport was the strongest recorded in central and eastern Montana. ||The strong winds caused at least one fire in Billings when a tree fell onto power lines near the intersection of Poly Drive and Hoover Avenue. The top of the downed tree burned briefly but the flames did not spread. The incident contributed to power outages for 800 to 1000 electric customers, mostly across the northwestern part of the city. Multiple power outages were reported in the Billings and Laurel areas throughout the day. The high winds also caused Zoo Montana to close after multiple trees fell on the property. In addition, minor house damage was also reported across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 59 mph was recorded at the Baker airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep plume of Pacific moisture overran a cold airmass east of the Continental Divide resulting in areas of heavy snow just before the Thanksgiving holiday. Snow amounts in excess of 6 inches were common. Gusty winds created areas of blowing and drifting snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six inches of snow fell at Lewistown 1SW.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep plume of Pacific moisture overran a cold airmass east of the Continental Divide resulting in areas of heavy snow just before the Thanksgiving holiday. Snow amounts in excess of 6 inches were common. Gusty winds created areas of blowing and drifting snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roads were snow-covered and icy causing hazardous travel conditions during a high-impact period of the Thanksgiving holiday travel period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system developed over the Great Basin and tracked northeastward into central and eastern Montana the first week of November. Heaviest snow amounts were in the mountains but the storm's impacts at lower elevations included power outages in Bozeman and numerous vehicle slide offs on area roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Homestake Pass on I-90 reporting hazardous travel conditions due to snow-covered and icy roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper jet moving along the US - Canadian border, along with an approaching upper trough from the west, resulted in strong surface winds across a large portion of North Central and Southwest Montana. Wind gusts in excess of 60 mph were common. The strong winds were responsible for blowing over two semi-trailer trucks at Mile Marker 355 on Interstate 15.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust to 61 mph was reported at Fort Belknap 13S (the Fort Belknap RAWS site).

The first verifying wind gust of 58 mph was measured at 5:46 pm at the Saco US-2 DOT site, while the peak wind gust of 62 mph was measured at 7:28 pm at the Glasgow Airport ASOS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A marginally unstable atmosphere combined with a weak upper-level disturbance to form some strong thunderstorms, a few of which became severe across portions of northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported a metal grain building blown down and scattered. Windows also reported broken.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A high wind event along the rocky mountain front and adjacent high plains. Strong winds aloft also were mixed down to the surface further into the plains on the 29th due to some convection that developed with peak heating.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter in Babb reported a 72 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A high wind event along the rocky mountain front and adjacent high plains. Strong winds aloft also were mixed down to the surface further into the plains on the 29th due to some convection that developed with peak heating.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dellwo MCSCN site reported a 69 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moist surface conditions, an unstable atmosphere, and a low pressure trough combined to form and sustain multiple organized severe thunderstorms across portions of northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported quarter-sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist surface layer combined with an unstable atmosphere and a passing upper-level disturbance to generate multiple moderately-strong and slow-moving thunderstorms with flooding concerns along the Yellowstone River Valley in Prairie County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Prairie County Fire Chief reported an inch of rain in only 15 minutes, flooded streets, sidewalks, and basements in the City of Terry, and flooding across Montana Interstate 94 near Sand Creek and Conns Coulee.  When storms finally ended after 2:00 am local time, rainfall totals of 5 to 6 inches were common.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Significant instability, a low pressure circulation over southeastern Montana, favorable winds, and warm, moist air all combined with an approaching strong upper-level storm system to quickly develop and maintain well-organized severe thunderstorms over many locations and a macroburst in the Glendive area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported quarter-sized hail with heavy rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An exceptionally strong atmospheric river brought up to 3 inches of precipitation and well-above normal temperatures to the region. Record-breaking temperatures into the 50s occurred in many valley locations. An unusually high number of roadway rockslides was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A rockslide was reported along US-2 just east of West Glacier, MT. The eastbound lane was blocked by softball-sized rocks. Road crews cleared debris and the road was usable again an hour later.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Peak heating and an approaching shortwave trough combined to create some strong thunderstorms and an isolated severe thunderstorm across Central Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 64 mph and half inch hail was reported. Some damage was done to buildings and trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated thunderstorm produced severe wind gusts across portions of Yellowstone County.

Significant instability, a low pressure circulation over southeastern Montana, favorable winds, and warm, moist air all combined with an approaching strong upper-level storm system to quickly develop and maintain well-organized severe thunderstorms over many locations and a macroburst in the Glendive area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported wind gusts up to 65 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wildfire occurred just west of Columbus roughly around noon on Saturday, September 19th.  Multiple resources that helped fight the fire were the Columbus, Park City, Nye and Absarokee Fire Departments, as well as the Carbon and Sweet Grass County DNRC. Fire suppression efforts from the ground and air with air tankers and helicopter drops were provided. The north end of the Countryman Creek Subdivision along Arrowhead Ridge Road, approximately five miles southwest of Columbus was evacuated. The fire threatened 15 to 20 homes with two of the homes being destroyed.  The wildfire, which was human caused, burned approximately 700 acres.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wildfire occurred just west of Columbus roughly around noon on Saturday, September 19th. Multiple resources that helped fight the fire were the Columbus, Park City, Nye and Absarokee Fire Departments, as well as the Carbon and Sweet Grass County DNRC. Fire suppression efforts from the ground and air with air tankers and helicopter drops were provided. The north end of the Countryman Creek Subdivision along Arrowhead Ridge Road, approximately five miles southwest of Columbus was evacuated. The fire threatened 15 to 20 homes with two of the homes being destroyed. The wildfire, which was human caused, burned approximately 700 acres.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper low moved across Wyoming during the late afternoon and early evening hours of the 5th. This resulted in warm, southerly winds across portions of South Central and Southeast Montana. In addition,  plenty of afternoon heating resulted in an unstable atmosphere ahead of a cold front that moved across the area during the early evening hours. Thunderstorms developed across Northern Wyoming and Southern Montana during the late afternoon hours and moved northeast across Southeast Montana during the evening. These thunderstorms produced primary large hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large and strong low pressure system from the pacific northwest moved through the northern Rockies and combined with an unstable atmosphere, spreading a few periods of strong to severe thunderstorms across portions of northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Glendive airport AWOS measured a 64 mph wind gust.  No damage was reported from anyone in town, and surrounding observations only had wind gusts in the 40-45 mph range with the storms as they passed through.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The center of a strong upper level ridge moved southeast towards Wyoming and allowed showers and thunderstorms to develop mainly from terrain influences.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A person from the public reported persistent rain and hail between 6:30pm to 7:30pm. They estimated the largest hail were quarter size.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Monday June 29th a thunderstorm hit Anaconda and produced some minor flooding. High intensity rain fell on nearly barren hillsides above the north side of town. Mud and water flowed down the slope and around 10 homes, a few of which sustained minor damage, and some water in their basements. An estimated 1 to 1.25 inches of rain fell between West Valley and Anaconda in about an hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms brought heavy rain just west of Anaconda. Edward from Anaconda reported 0.59 inches of rain and his friend who lives to the west recorded 1.13 inches of precipitation. The intense rain caused mud run off into a basement.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A favorable vertical wind profile and a persistent flow of moist and unstable air into the region allowed severe thunderstorms to be sustained well through the late night and overnight hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported 60 mph winds and 0.7 inches of rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A favorable vertical wind profile and a persistent flow of moist and unstable air into the region allowed severe thunderstorms to be sustained well through the late night and overnight hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A total of 19 three-phase poles and 11 single phase were reported damaged.  This included poles broken at ground level, as well as cross arms broken and wires snapped.  The radar indicated a bow echo through the area and wind speeds are estimated.  Damage is estimated at $75k to 80k.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Muddy Creek 2 Fire was a wildfire which was caused by lightning. The fire started on Saturday, July 25th, at approximately 2:45 PM and was located 5 miles southwest of Lame Deer, Montana. The wildfire burned approximately 2,322 acres, most of which was timber and grass. Total personnel on the fire was about 132. The fire was fully contained at approximately 12 AM Saturday, August 1st. More detailed information can be found at http://inciweb.nwcg.gov/.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Muddy Creek 2 Fire was a wildfire which was caused by lightning. The fire started on Saturday, July 25th, at approximately 2:45 PM and was located 5 miles southwest of Lame Deer, Montana. The wildfire burned approximately 2,322 acres, most of which was timber and grass. Total personnel on the fire was about 132. The fire was fully contained at approximately 12 AM Saturday, August 1st. More detailed information can be found at http://inciweb.nwcg.gov/.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Pine Ridge Wildfire was started by lightning on Sunday evening, July 26th, at about 5:45 PM and was located on the Crow Indian Reservation in South Central Montana. The wildfire burned approximately 1,700 acres after burning fiercely westward through grass and sagebrush toward the Little Big Horn River from its start north of Gas Cap Hill. The fire grew about 60 acres the afternoon of Monday, July 27th, as 56 mph winds accompanied a passing cold front, and was fully contained on July 29th. More detailed information can be found at http://inciweb.nwcg.gov/.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Pine Ridge Wildfire was started by lightning on Sunday evening, July 26th, at about 5:45 PM and was located on the Crow Indian Reservation in South Central Montana. The fire grew about 60 acres the afternoon of Monday, July 27th, as 56 mph winds accompanied a passing cold front, and was fully contained on July 29th. More detailed information can be found at http://inciweb.nwcg.gov/.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The National Weather Service in Glasgow conducted a damage survey in Glendive on July 28, 2015 following a significant severe thunderstorm that hit the evening before coming in from the southwest.  NWS staff met with county and city officials before and after the survey to be briefed and brief them on what was discovered.   The event was a macroburst (large scale straight line wind event) with some microburst components in between. | 	There were not enough time and resources to enter the various neighborhoods that were impacted, and due to the number of trees and power lines down, it was not safe to try and go around these areas where crews were working.  In order to best evaluate the wind speeds, the structures with the most damage were focused on as recommended by the Emergency Operations Center staff.   Several dozen homes were viewed though, and the damage was generally due to trees and branches lost, or shingles torn off of roofs.  That includes trees in homes or on cars, some partially blown off roofs, and trailer homes or semi-trailers blown over.  The damage survey started with the power lines west-northwest of town on Highway 200, then to the Glendive airport,  Seven Mile Drive, back through town on Highway 200S, up Merrill Ave and then up Highway 16 north to Sidney for 10 miles.  |	Access to the large metal truss electrical transmission towers west-northwest of town on Highway 200S was not possible due to the extreme mud conditions.  There were at least 5 noted as having crumbled toward the ground.   Further investigation will have to be done with the owner of the structures as to the age of these towers, but at minimum the winds would���ve been 120 mph.  There are also numerous wooden poles snapped at the bases and part way up along Highway 200.   The NWS will get total numbers from utility providers once they have a chance to assess all the damage.  |At the airport, winds were gusting to 90 mph last night on an anemometer on top of the terminal roof.   The winds tore the anemometer off the roof though, so a peak wind was not measured.  The FAA AWOS station that is the official observing equipment for the airport lost power, and a maximum gust was not received from it either.    There was a large cottonwood tree that was uprooted near the terminal building.  There were three metal roof/siding airplane hangars damaged.  One was completely destroyed, and the other two had the metal roofing peeled back/off.  The one that was destroyed wasn���t cemented into the ground and the posts easily ripped out of the ground when the wind blew against it.  All the debris was blown towards the north-northeast. The winds are estimated at 90 to 95 mph at this location.  |The next stop was on Highway 200S just southeast of the Interstate turnoff.  Significant damage occurred to a large metal building.  The failure point was two large garage doors that face southwest.  There is nothing to stop the wind as it rolls through the valley towards it.  The garage doors blew in, and the roof blew off.  One of the employees mentioned that sheet metal from another building southwest of them was blown towards their building.  The impact of that metal hitting the doors may have helped cause the doors to fail and be blown in.  This metal building had solid steel I-Beams in the ceiling that were bent back where the roof peeled back.    Power poles with transformers on them were snapped off near this facility and just east-southeast of it.  The transformers are pretty heavy and the resistance and weight of them against the wind made them easy targets to be snapped. All the debris was blown in a north-northeasterly direction.  Between the building and the power poles, winds at this location are estimated to have been around 100 to 110 mph.|7 Mile Drive was next.  There were many homes with shingles peeled off, and large trees split and large branches down, metal buildings damaged and sheet metal blown around. A few power poles were also snapped.  We stopped at an office building and a newer, well-built warehouse after noticing the garage doors on the warehouse were bent out.  There was also some insulation missing on a portion of a wall.   They had already cleaned up the south side doors, which had blown in as the winds came off the hill and crumbled some of the north side doors.  There was very little noticeable roof damage, which is something you will often see with garage doors blowing in, a testament to how well the building was built and the steel I-beams used in the trusses.  Winds at this location are estimated at 100-105 mph.     |Another metal building structure was viewed south of the interstate off of 7 Mile Drive.  A large overhead door that faces south was blown in and the roof right above it peeled off and blown to the north.   On the north side of the building, the majority of the roof (with wooden trusses) was either collapsed into the building or blown north-northeastward.  Winds at this location are estimated at 95 to 100 mph.|As we went down Highway 200S into downtown, the most notable damage was tree branches down.   We went down Marsh Road, and there were a lot of trees damaged in that area from 1 to 3 miles south of town.  House damage was generally due to branches falling on them or against them.   Heading north on Merrill Ave, tree damage was the most noticed damage, specifically near the high school football field.  A large blue spruce was noted down near I-94 and roof damage with shingles and fascia torn off were noted on a new hotel at that location.  Winds in this portion of town are estimated to have been 80-90 mph.  |We continued north on Highway 16 towards Sidney.  The number of damaged trees and sheds quickly reduced as we headed north and by 10 miles we were really looking hard to see any damage.  At four miles north of town, there was a home with a new barn that had all but the west wall collapse.  With no one home, we did not go out into the field to assess the barn closer, but it was newly built.  Winds at this point are estimated at 100 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very tight pressure gradient brought high winds to portions of Wheatland County.
A strong cold front combined with unstable conditions brought snow and windy conditions to the Judith Gap area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall around 4 inches combined with wind gusts 35 to 40 mph resulted in significantly reduced visibilities to the Judith Gap area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist and unstable upslope flow brought accumulating snow to the Red Lodge foothills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 6 to 9 inches were reported across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low and surface low situated across eastern Idaho and western Wyoming resulted in the first winter storm to portions of the Billings Forecast Area. Heavy snow accumulations occurred in the mountains with lesser amounts across the adjacent foothills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 6-8 inches were reported on the west side of the Crazy Mountains with 10 inches in Melville.

Damage included: a downed tree in Columbus, roof damage to the Harlowton courthouse, and a downed light pole in Worden.

An arctic front backed in from the northeast and moved westward across the Billings Forecast Area. A deep upslope flow was ushered in behind the front. This upslope flow combined with a strong, unstable disturbance aloft, resulted in heavy snow across the central and western portions of the area, as well as the mountain foothills locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ryegate received 6 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving, cold air mass over the region and the still un-frozen surface of Fort Peck Lake allowed for a few periods of lake fog to develop and drift across portions of northeast Montana, resulting in dense freezing fog.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This was the last lingering period of dense freezing fog from the same event that occurred over the last few days of November.  Visibility at 1/4 mile or less remained at the Poplar RAWS site through these overnight hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quickly-developing low pressure circulation emerged from the Canadian Rockies and strengthened over southern Saskatchewan during the late night and overnight hours.  With higher pressure over the Wyoming Rockies, the tight pressure gradient in between directed very strong and gusty winds through portions of northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds of at least 40 mph entered the western portion of the county and were recorded at the US-2 Saco DOT site as early as 12:17 AM on the 10th.  The first verifying wind gust of 58 mph was recorded there at 12:28 AM. The peak wind gust of 61 mph was recorded at 2:44 AM at the King Coulee RAWS in the southwestern portion of the county. Winds diminished just below criteria quickly thereafter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quickly-developing low pressure circulation emerged from the Canadian Rockies and strengthened over southern Saskatchewan during the late night and overnight hours.  With higher pressure over the Wyoming Rockies, the tight pressure gradient in between directed very strong and gusty winds through portions of northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On the morning of the 10th at the Sidney airport AWOS site, sustained winds of 40-43 mph were recorded from 4:35 AM to 5:35 AM.  Frequent gusts of near 40 to 54 mph were recorded from 3:15 AM to 9:35 AM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quickly-developing low pressure circulation emerged from the Canadian Rockies and strengthened over southern Saskatchewan during the late night and overnight hours. EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the Malta South US-191 DOT site, steady, sustained winds of at least 40 mph began at 5:50 PM on the 9th and ended around 2:40 AM on the 10th.  The peak wind gust of 70 mph was recorded multiple times, from 7:10 to 7:20 PM, then again at 8:21 PM all on the 9th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quickly-developing low pressure circulation emerged from the Canadian Rockies and strengthened over southern Saskatchewan during the late night and overnight hours. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pronounced low-level inversion and light or calm winds combined with sufficient moisture at the surface to allow widespread dense fog to form over portions of northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the Glendive airport AWOS site, on the 12th, visibilities of steadily a 1/4 mile or less began at 12:15 AM and lasted until 6:15 AM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The continued low-level inversion and light or calm winds combined with sufficient moisture at the surface to allow widespread dense fog to return over portions of northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the Glasgow airport ASOS site, visibilities steadily below a half mile lasted for 3 hours between 2:53 AM and 5:53 AM on the 12th.  Then during part of the day, some of the fog broke up partially but still remained generally patchy.  Dense fog returned during the afternoon hours of the 12th and continued until 1:53 AM on the 13th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: From the Plentywood mesonet site, visibilities less than a half mile were recorded from 6:00 PM on the 12th to 1:45 AM on the 13th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the early morning hours on November 10th, steady moderate snow fell in Butte from a winter storm embedded in a broad trough of low pressure. Although snowfall amounts were not extreme, antecedent moisture on paved surface froze before the snowfall and caused extremely slick conditions. Law enforcement agencies in the area had to call in additional help overnight to assist travelers who had slid off roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: During the overnight hours, law enforcement staff were overwhelmed by number of vehicle slide-offs. More than 12 weather-related crashes occurred in the vicinity of Butte. They needed to call in multiple off-duty staff members to assist in accident clean-up and in helping stranded motorists.  MDT road crews reported severe driving conditions on I-15 south of Feeley Hill interchange, with ice under the fresh snowfall. Chains were required for towing units, with lane blockages due to semi-trucks being unable to summit pass. One snow plow drifted off road in heavy snow conditions.  A storm spotter reported 7.0 inches of snow 7 miles southeast of Butte, MT over a 24 hour period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong jet aloft with a core wind speed of 170+ mph helped drive a fast moving cold front which brought high winds to many locations across the Northern Rockies. Snow above 5500 feet led to winter storm conditions for high passes and back-country users.  Lower atmosphere standardized wind anomalies ranged between +3 and +4 for this time of year, indicating a once-in-10-year wind event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power outages affected many communities in Lincoln County. Trees took out power lines and a 4 power poles.  Several pine, elm and spruce trees were blown down and blocking area roads. Prior rainfall contributed to helping push the trees over. One hotel had severe roof damage, and a few houses lost their metal roof, although these roofs were old and reportedly not up to code. Debris was widespread in county. Cell service was down from 7 pm 11/17 through 4 pm 11/18. In Troy, they lost power overnight. The winds in Libby were worse than measured at the nearby Libby airport.

Lower atmosphere standardized wind anomalies ranged between +3 and +4 for this time of year, indicating a once-in-10-year wind event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 1:30 am, the Missoula International Airport ASOS reported a wind gust of 59 mph. Tens of Ponderosa Pine and other deciduous trees in town were blown down overnight, and several limbs were blown off. A trained weather spotter estimated 60+ mph winds in southern Missoula around 1 am 12/18, additionally reporting roof damage to house due to 8-inch diameter limb falling off tree. An apartment complex in the hills of southern Missoula sustained heavy roof damage as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a strong cold front moved south out of Canada, low level convergence along the front produced areas of heavy snow. Snow accumulations in excess of 8 inches were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe driving conditions reported along I-15 from Glen to Feeley Hill.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a strong cold front moved south out of Canada, low level convergence along the front produced areas of heavy snow. Snow accumulations in excess of 8 inches were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three Forks School District reported that the Clarkston Bus Route would not be running due to unsafe road conditions. MDOT reporting hazardous travel conditions due to snow-covered and icy roads along US-287 near Townsend.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hazardous travel conditions due to snow-covered and icy roads along with low visibility in blowing snow, especially over Kings Hill Pass.

Emergency managers reported a street light blown down in Anaconda, with multiple trees down in the area. Additionally, a tree smashed a parked car in Butte, causing thousands of dollars of damage.
