Multiple trees were blown down over both lanes of US-2 near Essex, MT. High elevation RAWS sites reported winds gusts in the 50 mph range, causing dangerous back-county conditions for hunters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic front passed through western Montana during a very busy holiday travel week, occurring on the days immediately preceding Thanksgiving. Strong winds, snow, blowing snow, and flash-freezing of roadways impacted most of the Northern Rockies. Dangerous travel conditions with hundreds of stranded vehicles and crashes lead to the Missoula County Sheriff to declare emergency-only travel due to ���extreme hazardous weather conditions���. Other valleys saw similar conditions with multiple inches of snow coupled with breezy winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter in Troy reported 4 inches during the morning hours. Another spotter reported 3.5 inches of snow north of Noxon, MT. Roads crews reported black ice on roads causing hazardous driving conditions.

Seven slide-offs were recorded in the area throughout the day. Hwy 200 was especially bad during this storm, with several accidents and one semi truck getting stuck.

That evening, power was lost by about 8000 customers, as over a dozen power poles were blown down.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: For 9 hours, from 4pm 12/17 - 1am 12/18, the Pardee RAWS recorded gusts of around 40 mph, with a peak gust of 45 mph at 10pm. Sustained winds in the valleys were estimated at 45 mph based on damages. The relatively uncommon SSE wind direction likely contributed to tree damage with this storm. Five large trees fell near Alberton, MT, one which briefly blocked an interstate lane overnight. Additionally, Superior, MT schools were cancelled on 12/18 due to power outages.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 7 to 14 inches were reported across the area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were widespread spotter reports of 3 to 4 inches of snow in Missoula during the afternoon and evening of 11/24. The highest report was 7.2 inches in the upper South Hills neighborhood of Missoula. The Missoula County Sheriff's office requested emergency travel only due to extreme hazardous weather conditions. This is their highest level of travel alert, and a significant impact to travel during the evening commute. The general travel ban lasted 3 hrs, and during this time, vehicles and buses were unable to drive up to houses in the hills around Missoula due to slick roads. Over 40 accidents and slide-offs occurred in Missoula & Bitterroot Valleys during this time frame. Many schools cancelled class the next day due to continued slick roads.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The first period of dense freezing fog was recorded at the Wolf Point Airport AWOS site from 7:53 PM on the 29th to 12:53 AM on the 30th.  The second period of dense freezing fog was recorded at both the Wolf Point AWOS site and the Poplar RAWS site and generally lasted from 4:30 PM on the 30th through 11:59 PM on the 30th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: In general, the stronger winds began during the early morning on the 18th and ended during the early morning on the 19th.  The peak wind gust of 60 mph was recorded at the Beaver Hill I-94 DOT site at 1:53 PM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed low tracked east through southern Idaho on November 3rd, bringing snow and the first widespread travel impacts to Butte and southwest Montana for the 2015 - 2016 winter. Bert Mooney Airport in Butte, MT had to cancel multiple flights, and multiple accidents were reported from the slushy snow accumulations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roads were reported to have slushy accumulations of 3 to 6 inches in Butte, with at least 5 slide-offs accidents influenced by weather. Widespread storm accumulations of 3 to 6 inches were reported, with a highest estimated snow report of 7.0 inches 7 miles southeast of Butte, and the highest measured report of 6.5 inches 7 miles south of Butte. Additionally, the public reported 7 - 9 inches of snow over Homestake Pass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong low pressure system sliding down the Canadian Rockies, pushed up against higher pressure from the south and directed the resulting strong and very gusty winds through Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind at or above 40 mph began around mid-day and ended later that afternoon with a peak wind gust of 59 mph recorded at 1:18 PM at the Poplar RAWS site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong low pressure system sliding down the Canadian Rockies, pushed up against higher pressure from the south and directed the resulting strong and very gusty winds through Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds of 30 to 35 mph and gusts of 45 to 55 mph were more common across Central and Southern Valley County, but a sudden peak gust of 60 mph with the passage of the squall line was measured at the Glasgow Airport ASOS site at 3:03 AM.  Sustained winds steadily dropped back down below 40 mph by 12:30 PM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic front passed through western Montana during a very busy holiday travel week, occurring on the days immediately preceding Thanksgiving. Strong winds, snow, blowing snow, and flash-freezing of roadways impacted most of the Northern Rockies. Dangerous travel conditions with hundreds of stranded vehicles and crashes lead to the Missoula County Sheriff to declare emergency-only travel due to ���extreme hazardous weather conditions���. Other valleys saw similar conditions with multiple inches of snow coupled with breezy winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nine inches of snow fell at Lolo Pass during the event, with snow-covered roads and slick driving conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic front passed through western Montana during a very busy holiday travel week, occurring on the days immediately preceding Thanksgiving. Strong winds, snow, blowing snow, and flash-freezing of roadways impacted most of the Northern Rockies. Dangerous travel conditions with hundreds of stranded vehicles and crashes lead to the Missoula County Sheriff to declare emergency-only travel due to ���extreme hazardous weather conditions���. Other valleys saw similar conditions with multiple inches of snow coupled with breezy winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters reported between 3 to 5.8 inches of snow in Kalispell during the day, with drifts up to a foot and a half deep. Winds at the Glacier Park International Airport ASOS gusted to around 40 mph during the morning and afternoon, peaking at 41 mph in the late morning. Roads became very slick by the afternoon. Local law enforcement reported that snow and wind had caused over 60 vehicle accidents or slide-offs in the Flathead Valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moved through the area bringing moderate to heavy snow to the mountains while valley locations saw a transition to freezing rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lookout Pass on Interstate 90 saw heavy snow that caused reduced visibility. The ski resort by the pass reported 7 inches total snowfall for this event. MDT reported snow and blowing snow with reduced visibility between Tarkio and Alberton. A person from the public reported very slick conditions in the St. Regis area with several vehicle slideoffs reported. There were reports of freezing rain towards Missoula and a tractor trailer spun out in the driving lane of US-93 at Evaro Hill before daybreak.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moved through the area bringing moderate to heavy snow to the mountains while valley locations saw a transition to freezing rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light freezing rain with slick roadways were reported between Lolo and Missoula on US-93, on the Eastside Highway between Stevensville and Florence. Also freezing rain was reported in Condon along Highway 83. Snow amounts across western Montana included 8.2 Seeley Lake, 6 Bull Lake, 4 Greenough and 3 in both Libby and Stevensville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong low pressure system sliding down the Canadian Rockies, pushed up against higher pressure from the south and directed the resulting strong and very gusty winds through Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the Scobey Airport mesonet site, sustained winds of 40 to 41 mph were recorded  for 2 to 3 hours through the middle portion of the day. Event times are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong westerly flow aloft combined with the passage of several shortwaves, resulted in a tight surface pressure gradient and high winds over a large portion of North Central Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust to 63 mph was reported at McAllister 4NNE (the Norris Hill DOT site).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong westerly flow aloft combined with the passage of several shortwaves, resulted in a tight surface pressure gradient and high winds over a large portion of North Central Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust to 58 mph was reported at Cut Bank 3SW (the Cut Bank ASOS site).

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust to 58 mph was reported at Augusta 12NNE.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust to 67 mph was reported at White Sulphur Springs 1SE.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong westerly flow aloft combined with the passage of several shortwaves, resulted in a tight surface pressure gradient and high winds over a large portion of North Central Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust to 76 mph was reported at Cascade.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong westerly flow aloft combined with the passage of several shortwaves, resulted in a tight surface pressure gradient and high winds over a large portion of North Central Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust to 68 mph was reported at Helena 3ENE (the Helena ASOS site).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic front backed in from the northeast and moved westward across the Billings Forecast Area. A deep upslope flow was ushered in behind the front. This upslope flow combined with a strong, unstable disturbance aloft, resulted in heavy snow across the central and western portions of the area, as well as the mountain foothills locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 6 to 10 inches was reported across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic front backed in from the northeast and moved westward across the Billings Forecast Area. A deep upslope flow was ushered in behind the front. This upslope flow combined with a strong, unstable disturbance aloft, resulted in heavy snow across the central and western portions of the area, as well as the mountain foothills locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 6-9 inches was reported across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a strong cold front moved south out of Canada, low level convergence along the front produced areas of heavy snow. Snow accumulations in excess of 8 inches were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nine inches of snow fell at Pony 5SW. Also, 6 inches fell at Ennis.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of colder air moving in behind a cold front, strong pressure rises and showers and thunderstorms brought strong winds to western Montana. The standardized wind speed anomaly from approximately nine thousand feet MSL. was between +2 and +3.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very strong winds took three power poles down, some of which were splintered, along Maclay Ranch Rd. south of Trader Brothers three miles south of Lolo. A nearby resident south of the power pole report experienced tiles being blown off their roof. A south Missoula resident located near the Linda Vista area reported 5 rows of roof shingles lost. A private weather station located southeast of Lolo at 3632 ft. reported at wind gust of 57 mph at 11:38 am and 59 mph at 12:39 pm MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A system digging south out of Canada accompanied by a strong upper level jet produced intense snow showers along Flathead Lake.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weather spotter reported heavy snowfall with reduced visibility and snow covered Highway 35 near Bigfork. A weather spotter located 8 miles north of Finley Point measured 4.5 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep plume of Pacific moisture overran a cold airmass east of the Continental Divide resulting in areas of heavy snow just before the Thanksgiving holiday. Snow amounts in excess of 6 inches were common. Gusty winds created areas of blowing and drifting snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roads were snow-covered and icy causing hazardous travel conditions during a high-impact period of the Thanksgiving holiday travel period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system passed over the central and northern Rockies, directing moisture and cold air to favor accumulating snow across portions of northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A heavier band of snow lingered longer over southern Petroleum county and resulted in a public report of 12 inches of snow in the town of Winnett.  Event times are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system passed over the central and northern Rockies, directing moisture and cold air to favor accumulating snow across portions of northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snowfall reports of 7 inches were received both from a cooperative observer located at the Burke Ranch in the far southwestern corner of Valley County and from a NWS employee at Glasgow.  Event times are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a strong cold front moved south out of Canada, low level convergence along the front produced areas of heavy snow. Snow accumulations in excess of 8 inches were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hazardous travel conditions due to snow-covered and icy roads along with significant blowing and drifting snow near Denton. Great Falls Tribune was unable to be delivered to Raynesford, Geyser, Denton, Moore and Lewistown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a strong cold front moved south out of Canada, low level convergence along the front produced areas of heavy snow. Snow accumulations in excess of 8 inches were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six inches of snow fell at Garneill 12 ENE (the Crystal Lake SNOTEL site). Also, 7 inches fell at Grass Range.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough brought moderate to heavy snow and blowing snow to mountain passes in western Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow with with blowing snow caused by wind gusts to 30 mph and low visibility created hazardous conditions at Lookout Pass. Nine inches of snow was reported at the pass.  Chains were required for towing units at Lookout Pass and also from Taft to Haugen on Interstate 90.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough brought moderate to heavy snow and blowing snow to mountain passes in western Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lost Trail Pass received 21 inches of snow with periods of blowing snow during this event. Wind gusts to 25 mph were reported at 10 pm MST on the 12th which contributed to lower visibility from the falling and blowing snow. Snow plows had a hard time keeping up at times at Lost Trail Pass. Lolo Pass only received around 5 inches of snow with this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually strong Pacific front blasted across the Billings Forecast Area on December 9th. Tight pressure gradients ahead of the front resulted in high winds through the Livingston area and adjacent foothills of the Beartooth/Absaroka Mountains. Strong pressure rises were noted behind the front which brought another round of high winds to these areas, as well as areas further east across the lower elevations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts ranged from 63 mph to 74 mph across the Red Lodge Foothills.

At MacDonald Pass 22 inches of snow was estimated while 10 inches was measured in Elliston. A person from the public reported 16 inches, 7 miles southeast of Elliston. An estimated 18 inches of snow fell in Maxville while 8 inches was reported at deer Lodge.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Potomac, 4 inches of snow was reported while 2 inches fell in Seeley Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system and associated strong jet brought winds and snow to much of the Northern Rockies. Northwest Montana, including the Flathead Valley saw significant 12-hour snowfall totals overnight, with many locations receiving 6 to 12 inches of snow. Also this snowfall occurred during the pre-holiday period which elevated the impact to travel.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter located 6 miles northeast of Heron measured a storm total snowfall of 13 inches. Other amounts include 12 inches a few miles north of Libby, 7 inches at Troy and 6 inches in Libby.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Deer Lodge AGRIMET sensor reported a wind gust to 65 mph around 1:30 pm MST. The Bert Mooney Airport located south-southeast of Butte reported a gust to 55 mph at 107 pm MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system and associated strong jet brought winds and snow to much of the Northern Rockies. Northwest Montana, including the Flathead Valley saw significant 12-hour snowfall totals overnight, with many locations receiving 6 to 12 inches of snow. Also this snowfall occurred during the pre-holiday period which elevated the impact to travel.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Anywhere from 6 to 12 inches of snow fell across the Flathead to Mission Valley according to MDT. Up to 12 inches was reported along highway 206 between the junction with MT-35 and old US-2. Glacier Park International Airport observers measured a storm total snowfall of 9.5 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system and associated strong jet brought winds and snow to much of the Northern Rockies. Northwest Montana, including the Flathead Valley saw significant 12-hour snowfall totals overnight, with many locations receiving 6 to 12 inches of snow. Also this snowfall occurred during the pre-holiday period which elevated the impact to travel.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lolo Pass on US-12 & Lost Trail Pass on US-93 saw periods of moderate to heavy snow throughout this event. Moderate to heavy snow showers caused intermittent high impact into the afternoon of the 22nd. Lolo Pass saw a total of 8 to 10 inches from this storm while Lost Trail saw 8 inches. A tree fell across US-12 about 24 miles west of Lolo which caused a lane blockage, power line damage and power outages from approximately 834 pm through 3 am on the 22nd. A NWS employee reported 1 inch per hour snow rates at Lolo Pass at 12 pm on the 22nd.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell at Lookout Pass with 16 inches being reported by the nearby ski resort. Chains were required for all towing vehicles from Saltese to Lookout Pass on I-90. Several semi-trucks became stuck which caused lane blockage and subsequent road closure and delays around 8 pm on the 21st. Snow plows had a tough time keeping up with the snow.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Anywhere from 3 to 15 inches of snow fell along US-2 from West Glacier to Marias Pass according to the MDT. Chains were required for towing units for this region during the event. A railroad avalanche forecaster measured 15.6 inches of snow in the Essex area from this storm. Despite the remote extreme amounts, hardy folks reportedly were used to these amounts.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A gust of 58 mph was recorded at the Roscoe DOT site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms developed at 3 pm MDT over Lemhi county and was associated with very frequent lightning strikes. This cluster of thunderstorms developed into a line and brought strong damaging winds to the Bitterroot and Missoula valleys. Numerous power lines were knocked down as a line of 60 mph winds push through the Bitterroot valley. As the thunderstorms approached Missoula, a macroburst developed and brought 74 mph wind gusts to the city of Missoula. Many power lines and trees were brought down and caused power outages for days in some areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A fence with 5 x 5 inch posts was blown down. Wind estimates near 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic front backed in from the northeast and moved westward across the Billings Forecast Area. A deep upslope flow was ushered in behind the front. This upslope flow combined with a strong, unstable disturbance aloft, resulted in heavy snow across the central and western portions of the area, as well as the mountain foothills locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 6 to 11 inches was reported across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm and moist air moved into the Northern Rockies from subtropical latitudes ahead of an upper level trough. This in turn caused freezing rain due to below freezing temperatures in western Montana Valleys. The Montana Department of Transportation labeled many roads across northwest Montana to have severe driving conditions which is the severest category for them.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The MDT reported severe driving conditions starting at 11:25 pm on the 3rd and closed Interstate-90 from Haugen to St. Regis from 11:55 pm on the 3rd through 8 am on the 4th because of ice. MDT also reported severe driving conditions along Highway 200 from the Idaho border to Dixon. There were 47 automobile wrecks reported from Missoula to St. Regis according to a Facebook report.  Ice accretion amounts generally were around a tenth of an inch or less from St. Regis to Lookout Pass. The St. Regis, Superior and Trout Creek schools were closed due to the highway closures.

This in turn caused freezing rain due to below freezing temperatures in western Montana Valleys. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread severe driving conditions were reported for much of the overnight hours due to freezing rain. A spotter in Libby reported a thin glaze of ice coated on the driveway at 844 am on the 4th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lee-side trough across Southern Montana resulted in tight pressure gradients across the Upper Yellowstone and Stillwater Valleys. These tight pressure gradients resulted in the development of gap flow winds through a narrow corridor of Livingston. A deep, fresh snow cover combined with winds that gusted around 60 mph resulted in a ground blizzard across the area. Winds gusted in the 50 to 65 mph range. These winds produced considerable blowing and drifting snow with near zero visibilities at times. Numerous cars got stuck in driftts and  multiple semitrailers slid off the interstate. The Montana Department of Transportation closed Interstate 90 from mile marker 330 to 337 and traffic was diverted through Livingston.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A general orientation of higher pressure to the north and lower pressure to the south set up a favorable atmosphere to allow stronger winds to funnel between them and strengthen on the downwind slopes of the Little Rocky Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the Malta South US-191 DOT site, hourly sustained winds of at least 40 mph began during the late night hours and continued overnight with a peak wind gust of 58 mph at 12:39 AM.  Sustained winds diminished to below 40 mph after 1:20 AM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A snowmobiler died in an avalanche on Saturday, December 19th outside Cooke City. The avalanche occurred on the southeast face of Sheep Mountain between Lulu Pass and Round Lake. The slide was triggered from low on the slope by the victim who was subsequently buried six fee deep. The victim had an airbag but was unable to deploy it. The slide also caught two other party members who were parked in the run out zone. One was buried up to his chest while the other was completely buried. They both deployed their air bags and escaped without injury. For a more detailed report, please see the avalanche incidents report at http://www.mtavalanche.com.

A cluster of thunderstorms developed at 3 pm MDT over Lemhi county and was associated with very frequent lightning strikes. This cluster of thunderstorms developed into a line and brought strong damaging winds to the Bitterroot and Missoula valleys. Numerous power lines were knocked down as a line of 60 mph winds push through the Bitterroot valley. As the thunderstorms approached Missoula, a macroburst developed and brought 74 mph wind gusts to the city of Missoula. Many power lines and trees were brought down and caused power outages for days in some areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm unleashed a small microburst that caused hundreds of trees to either snap in half or blow over at the Denna Mora Rest Area on Interstate 90 east of Lookout Pass.  Two large fir and spruce trees with diameters up to 3 feet wide fell onto the eastbound lanes just to the northwest of the rest area obstructing the road at night. Approximately an hour later around 10 pm MDT, several vehicles hit the trees causing car damage, injuries and one fatality. A 21 year old male died after his vehicle collided with the topmost of the crisscrossed trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough to the west with strong surface southerly flow led to a favorable severe weather setup. Mostly clear skies over southwest Montana aided in surface heating and storms began to form by 2pm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public tweeted image confirms at least quarter size hail.
